text: Cover letter structure: exactly 3 paragraphs. P1: who you are and why this role (2 sentences). P2: proof from resume — name 2 real projects or experiences (3 sentences max). P3: clean close with concrete ask (1 sentence). Total: 120-150 words hard limit.

Cover letter tone: plain everyday English. Short sentences. No adjectives about yourself — let the work prove it. Avoid: passionate, excited, thrilled, eager, leverage, synergy, spearhead, results-driven, team player, quick learner, dynamic, hardworking, proven track record.

Strong cover letter opening — sentence 1: state your degree or most recent role plainly. Sentence 2: name one specific thing from the JD that made you apply — a technology, a product type, or a problem the company solves. Never write 'I am interested in this opportunity.'

Cover letter proof paragraph: name 2 real projects or experiences. For each say what was built and which JD requirement it covers. Be blunt and factual. 'Built X using Y, which covers Z requirement from the JD' is better than vague claims.

Cover letter close: state one concrete thing the candidate brings to this team. End with what they want — a quick call or an interview. No 'thank you for your time.' No 'I look forward to hearing from you.' Just a clean direct sentence.

Cover letter word count: 120-150 words total. Count before returning. Recruiters spend 20 seconds on a cover letter — shorter is always better. Every sentence must earn its place. Remove any sentence that does not add new information.

Cover letter ATS tips: use exact JD phrasing for role title and key technologies. Include the job title in the first paragraph. Use the company name only if specifically relevant to the content — never just to show you know the company name.

Fresher cover letter: lead with your most recent project or internship. Do not apologize for lack of experience. Frame education and projects as direct evidence of capability. Early-career candidates who lead with proof outperform those who lead with enthusiasm.
